rulings.Case Title: 690 MARKET CLUB OWNERS ASSOCIATION VS. JOE LEE RUNYON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Marriotts Notice Of Motion And Motion To Appoint General Judicial Referee Pursuant To Contract
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, March 26, 2015, Line 12, CROSS DEFENDANT MARRIOTT OWNERSHIP RESORTS, INC. Marriott's Motion To Appoint General Judicial Referee Pursuant To Contract. The motion for reference is granted and this case is stayed. CCP 638 is a legislatively prescribed procedure where parties may waive their right to a jury trial. In Grafton Partners, L.P. v. Sup. Crt. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 944, 959-961, the court explains that CCP 631 has no bearing on the validity of a contractual reference provision. The court has discretion to deny reference, but cannot do so arbitrarily. There is no unconscionability and the referee can fairly apportion the fees. The court denies the fee request. The prevailing party to the "litigation" is entitled to fees under the contract. This motion is merely one aspect of the litigation. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: SAM TRINH VS. AMEN CLINIC et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, March 26, 2015, Line 5, DEFENDANT THE PERMANENTE MEDICAL GROUP MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. The court denies the motion for summary judgment. Defendant has maintained its summary judgment burden of production based on the declaration of Dr. Nickles. He states that Dr. Wong complied with the standard of care. Plaintiff must then present competent medical expert testimony to avoid summary judgment. See Munro v. UC Regents, (1989) 215 Cal.App.3d 977, 985 ("When a defendant moves for summary judgment and supports his motion with expert declarations that his conduct fell within the community standard of care, he is entitled to summary judgment unless the plaintiff comes forward with conflicting expert evidence."). The court admits the declaration of Dr. Dusay and denies summary judgment. At par. 10 of his declaration, Dr. Dusay explains that based on Mr. Trinh's depression and anxiety, which Kaiser Permanente was aware of, and the warnings on the medication, Dr. Wong breached the standard of care. Dr. Wong was not merely acting as a pharmacist; he was treating Mr. Trinh. Given the conflicting expert opinions, a triable issue of material fact is presented as to breach of the standard of care. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: KATHERINE NG et al VS. CALIFORNIA PARKING COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Katherine Ng To Complete Mental Examination [Ccp 2023.020(C)]
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday March 26, 2015, Line 1, DEFENDANT KEITH ARMSTRONG, CALIFORNIA PARKING COMPANY'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff Katherine Ng To Complete Mental Examination [Ccp 2023.020(C)]. Pro Tem Judge Rebecca Woodson, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants' Motion to Compel Plaintiff Katherine Ng to Complete Mental Examination is GRANTED. The Court orders the parties to meet and confer prior to hearing on the matter and advise the Court of (1) a date and time certain to complete the MCMI-III psychological test by Dr. Carol Beebe Walser, Ph.D., and (2) the estimated time agreed upon by the parties to complete this test. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rwoodson@mckennalong.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KATHERINE NG et al VS. CALIFORNIA PARKING COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Plaintiffs Motion To Disqualify Defendants Expert Carol Beebe Walser, Ph.D.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday March 26, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFF KATHERINE NG, RICKY NG'S Motion To Plaintiffs Motion To Disqualify Defendants Expert Carol Beebe Walser, Ph.D. Pro Tem Judge Rebecca Woodson, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiffs' Motion to Disqualify Defendants' Expert Carol Beebe Walser, Ph.D. is DENIED. The Court finds that plaintiffs' have failed to provide "substantial evidence" that Dr. Walser was "repeatedly uncooperative" during Ms. Ng's IME. See Waicis v. Superior Court (1990) 226 Cal.App.3d 283. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rwoodson@mckennalong.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
